docs.title: 🟠 LazyFlare
docs.text: Cloudflare in your terminal Browse DNS, Workers, monitor KV Storage, and view resources - all from your terminal without opening a browser.

docs.title: No context switching
docs.text: Stay in your terminal while managing Cloudflare. No browser tabs, no slow dashboard clicks.

docs.title: Fast navigation
docs.text: Vim-style keys mean muscle memory works here too. Navigate zones, workers, and records in seconds.

docs.title: Real-time actions
docs.text: Toggle proxy status, purge cache, and delete records instantly. Filter and search through live data with a single keystroke.

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Start
docs.text:
# Recommended
pipx install lazyflare

# macOS/Linux
brew tap akhil-krishna-02/tap
brew install lazyflare

# Start it up
lazyflare

docs.title: Cloudflare Dashboard vs LazyFlare
docs.text: Task Cloudflare Web Console LazyFlare Switch between domains Click through slow menus j/k + l Browse KV Namespaces Click, wait for page load, click l to expand, h to collapse Purge Zone Cache Navigate to Caching > Config > Purge Select Zone + p Toggle DNS Proxy Edit Record, Click Toggle, Save Select Record + Space Search DNS Records Type in search bar, wait / + Instant in-memory filter Copy Record ID/Name Select, copy c key

docs.title: Ready to try it?
docs.text:
Install LazyFlare and start managing your Cloudflare edge in seconds.

Get Started{ .md-button .md-button--primary } GitHub{ .md-button }

docs.title: Mock Mode
docs.text: If you are a developer looking to contribute to the LazyFlare source code, or if you simply want to test the UI without risking any changes to your actual production Cloudflare infrastructure, we built Mock Mode for you.

docs.title: How to use Mock Mode
docs.text:
From anywhere within the application, simply press:

m

You will instantly see the Mode: indicator in the top header change from [Real] to [Mock] (highlighted in orange).

docs.title: What happens in Mock Mode?
docs.text:
When Mock Mode is engaged: 1. Network Requests Stop: LazyFlare immediately stops sending HTTP requests to the Cloudflare API. 2. Fake Data is Generated: As you navigate between Resources (like DNS Zones or Workers), the application procedurally generates fake infrastructure data (e.g. site-1.com, worker-service-123) to populate the tables. 3. Safe Mutations: You can safely test destructive actions! Pressing d to delete a record or Space to toggle proxy statuses will visually update the UI and print success messages to the Log, but your real Cloudflare account will remain completely untouched.

To return to your live Cloudflare infrastructure, simply press m again.

docs.title: Cache Management
docs.text: Clearing the Cloudflare Edge Cache is notoriously annoying to do via the web dashboard when deploying a quick hotfix to your production site. LazyFlare solves this by turning it into a single keystroke.

docs.title: Purging the Zone Cache
docs.text:
To instantly purge everything across all Edge nodes for a specific zone:

  1. Navigate to the DNS Zones resource.
  2. Select the domain you want to purge (e.g., mysite.com).
  3. Press p (for Purge).
  4. A large red confirmation modal will pop up warning you that you are about to purge the entire zone cache.
  5. Press Enter (Proceed).

You will immediately see a ✔ Cache Purged via API! message in the Commands & Logs panel in the bottom right, confirming that the Cloudflare edge network has successfully dumped your cached assets.

docs.title: DNS & Zones
docs.text: Managing DNS records in the web dashboard often involves clicking through multiple nested menus and waiting for slow table loads. LazyFlare brings all of your DNS records directly into your terminal's memory for instant manipulation.

docs.title: 🟠 Toggling Proxy Status
docs.text:
One of the most common tasks in Cloudflare is toggling the proxy status (turning the Cloudflare proxy off to expose the raw origin IP for debugging, or turning it back on for protection).

  1. Select DNS Zones from the left panel.
  2. Select your domain.
  3. Use j/k to highlight the specific DNS Record (e.g., your A record).
  4. Press Space.

The proxy status indicator will instantly flip between 🟠 (Proxied) and ⚪ (DNS Only), and the API call will execute in the background.

docs.title: Deleting Records
docs.text:
To clean up old or unused DNS records:

  1. Highlight the record in the Details panel.
  2. Press d.
  3. A confirmation modal will appear. Press Enter (Proceed) to safely delete the record from Cloudflare.

docs.title: Filtering & Search
docs.text:
When managing enterprise Cloudflare accounts with thousands of DNS records or hundreds of Workers, finding the exact resource you need can be tedious.

LazyFlare solves this with instantaneous, in-memory filtering.

docs.title: How to Filter
docs.text:
From anywhere in the app: 1. Press / to focus the Filter Bar (located right above the data table). 2. Begin typing.

As you type, the massive data table below will instantly re-render to only show rows that contain your search query. The search is case-insensitive and scans across all columns simultaneously.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: If you are viewing a DNS Zone with 500 records: - Type A to see all A records. - Type mysubdomain to find the exact record pointing to that subdomain. - Type 192.168. to find all records resolving to that specific IP subnet.

docs.title: Clearing the Filter
docs.text: To exit the filter bar and reset the data table back to its original state, simply press Esc.

docs.title: KV & D1 Storage
docs.text: Cloudflare's edge storage solutions (KV and D1) are fully accessible within LazyFlare.

docs.title: KV Store (Key-Value)
docs.text:
LazyFlare allows you to easily drill down into your global, low-latency KV namespaces.

  1. Select KV Store from the main navigation.
  2. Highlight a specific Namespace (e.g., user_sessions) and press l (or Enter) to Drill Down.
  3. The Details panel will immediately update to show the individual Keys inside that namespace.
  4. Press h to collapse back to the list of namespaces.

docs.title: Deleting Namespaces
docs.text: If you need to drop an old KV Namespace, you can highlight it from the main list and press d.

docs.title: D1 Databases
docs.text:
D1 is Cloudflare's serverless SQL database.

Selecting D1 Databases will instantly list out all of your provisioned SQL databases across your account, including their UUIDs and current internal versions.

Pressing c on a D1 row will instantly copy its UUID, which is highly useful when you need to quickly bind a new database to your wrangler.toml file!

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: LazyFlare uses a heavily optimized 2-panel layout designed for maximum speed and minimal context switching.

docs.title: The Layout
docs.text:
1. Resources Panel (Left): This is your main navigation hub. It lists all the available Cloudflare services you can interact with (Workers, DNS Zones, KV Store, etc.).
  2. Details Panel (Right): This massive data table displays the actual records, scripts, and namespaces for the selected resource.
  3. Commands & Logs (Bottom Right): A sticky terminal output that shows real-time success or failure messages from the Cloudflare API when you execute commands.

docs.title: Moving Around
docs.text:
We mapped the navigation completely to Vim keybindings to ensure your hands never have to leave the home row.

  • j - Move the cursor down one row.
  • k - Move the cursor up one row.
  • h - Go Back / Collapse out of a detailed view (e.g., returning from KV keys back to KV namespaces).
  • l - Drill Down / Expand a selected resource (e.g., opening a KV namespace to see its keys).
  • Tab - Instantly switch focus between the Left Panel and the Right Panel.

If you ever get lost or need to quickly jump to the search bar, you can press /. To clear your search and reset the view, just press Esc.

docs.title: Workers & Pages
docs.text: Cloudflare's serverless edge compute environment is incredibly powerful. LazyFlare gives you instant visibility into your deployments.

docs.title: Workers
docs.text:
Select Workers from the left panel to instantly load a list of all deployed worker scripts on your account.

LazyFlare will display: - The ID of the worker. - The Modified timestamp, so you can easily see when your last CI/CD deployment finished pushing to the edge.

docs.title: Pages Projects
docs.text:
Select Pages Projects from the navigation to view your hosted Jamstack applications.

LazyFlare lists: - The internal project Name. - The live URL (subdomain.pages.dev). - The Created date.

You can press c on any Pages project to instantly copy its live .pages.dev URL to your clipboard for quick testing in your browser!

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: LazyFlare requires a Cloudflare API Token to communicate with your account. It uses this token securely and never logs it or sends it anywhere other than the official Cloudflare API.

docs.title: 1. Generating an API Token
docs.text:
1. Go to your Cloudflare Dashboard Profile.
  2. Click Create Token.
  3. Scroll down and click Create Custom Token.
  4. Give it a memorable name like LazyFlare CLI.
  5. Under Permissions, grant Edit (or at least Read) access to the resources you want to manage. For the full experience, we recommend:
  6. Zone -> DNS -> Edit
  7. Zone -> Cache Purge -> Purge
  8. Account -> Workers Scripts -> Read (or Edit)
  9. Account -> Workers KV Storage -> Read (or Edit)
  10. Click Continue to summary and then Create Token.
  11. Copy the token. You will only be shown this once!

docs.title: 2. Setting the Token
docs.text: LazyFlare supports two methods for securely supplying your token.

docs.title: Method A: Configuration File (Recommended)
docs.text:
This is the safest method as it keeps the token out of your shell history and profile scripts. LazyFlare looks for a configuration file located at ~/.config/lazyflare/config.

Run the following commands in your terminal:

mkdir -p ~/.config/lazyflare
echo "CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN=your_super_secret_token_here" > ~/.config/lazyflare/config

docs.title: Method B: Environment Variable
docs.text:
If you prefer managing secrets via your shell environment (e.g., in your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc), you can export the token directly:

export CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN="your_super_secret_token_here"

docs.title: 3. Running the App
docs.text:
Once your token is configured via either method, simply run:

lazyflare

You should see the application boot up, authenticate, and display your Account ID in the top left corner!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: LazyFlare is distributed as a standard Python package, making it easy to install across macOS, Linux, and Windows.

docs.title: Using pipx (Recommended)
docs.text:
pipx is the recommended way to install Python CLI applications because it installs them in isolated environments, avoiding conflicts with other Python packages on your system.

pipx install lazyflare

If you don't have pipx installed, you can install it via:

# macOS
brew install pipx
pipx ensurepath

# Linux/Windows
python -m pip install --user pipx
python -m pipx ensurepath

docs.title: Using Homebrew (macOS / Linux)
docs.text:
If you prefer managing your packages via Homebrew, we provide a custom tap:

brew tap akhil-krishna-02/tap
brew install lazyflare

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
If you want to contribute to the project or run the absolute latest development version, you can install directly from the source code:

git clone https://github.com/akhil-krishna-02/lazyflare.git
cd lazyflare
pip install -e .

docs.title: Verifying the Installation
docs.text:
Once installed, you can verify that it is working by running:

lazyflare --help

You should see the UI launch (or show an authentication warning if you haven't configured your token yet).

Next Step: Head over to Configuration to set up your Cloudflare API token.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Follow these steps to get LazyFlare running on your machine.

docs.title: 1. Installation
docs.text:
Using pipx (Recommended)

pipx install lazyflare

Using Homebrew (macOS / Linux)

brew tap akhil-krishna-02/tap
brew install lazyflare

docs.title: 2. Generate a Cloudflare API Token
docs.text: You need a Custom API Token from Cloudflare to allow LazyFlare to read and write to your edge network. 1. Go to your Cloudflare Dashboard Profile. 2. Click Create Token -> Custom Token. 3. Grant Edit permissions for the following: - Zone DNS - Cache Purge - Workers Scripts - Workers KV Storage

docs.title: 3. Configuration
docs.text:
LazyFlare natively supports configuration files. This prevents your secrets from cluttering your terminal history or .bashrc.

Create the configuration directory:

mkdir -p ~/.config/lazyflare

Save your token in ~/.config/lazyflare/config:

CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN="your_super_secret_token_here"

docs.title: 4. Run LazyFlare
docs.text:
Just launch the app from your terminal!

lazyflare

docs.title: Keybindings
docs.text: LazyFlare is heavily inspired by Vim and lazygit, focusing entirely on keyboard-driven navigation so you never have to reach for your mouse.

docs.title: Global Navigation
docs.text: Key Action j / ↓ Move down in list k / ↑ Move up in list h / ← Go Back / Collapse current view l / → Drill Down / Expand selected resource Tab Switch focus between panels / Focus the Filter / Command Bar ? Show Help menu overlay

docs.title: Resource Actions
docs.text:
These commands act directly upon the item you currently have highlighted in the Details panel.

Key Action Context Enter Open resource / Fetch data All Space Toggle DNS Proxy Status (🟠/⚪) DNS Zones p Purge entire Zone Cache DNS Zones d Delete Item (prompts confirmation) DNS, KV c Copy Item ID/Name to clipboard All

docs.title: System Commands
docs.text: Key / Command Action q or /quit Exit LazyFlare r or /ref Refresh data from Cloudflare API m or /mock Toggle Mock Mode (Useful for testing offline)
